diagrams.title: Iron Triangle (Project Constraint Model)
diagrams.description: The Iron Triangle shows three interdependent project constraints: Scope (work to be done), Time (schedule/deadline), and Cost (budget/resources). In the center is Quality. Changing one constraint forces adjustments to others to maintain balance.
elements: Scope (left vertex) - defines what will be delivered, features, functionality
Time (right vertex) - project schedule, deadlines, milestones
Cost (top vertex) - budget, resources, team size
Quality (center) - affected by any constraint change
Arrows connecting vertices showing interdependence
Diagonal arrows representing trade-offs
diagrams.purpose: Visualize project constraint trade-offs. When one constraint changes (e.g., scope increases), the other two must adjust (more time, more cost, or reduced quality) to maintain balance. Essential for stakeholder negotiation and realistic planning.

diagrams.title: Project Lifecycle and PMBOK Process Groups
diagrams.description: The project lifecycle shows five process groups that overlap throughout the project. Initiating begins the project; Planning defines scope; Executing does the work; Monitoring & Controlling runs parallel to Executing; Closing ends the project. Each group has inputs, tools, techniques, and outputs.
elements: Time axis (horizontal)
Project phases (conception, development, implementation, close)
Process groups: Initiating (small at start), Planning (grows), Executing (largest), Monitoring & Controlling (parallel to Executing), Closing (tapers)
Feedback loops from Monitoring back to earlier phases
Deliverables output at each phase
diagrams.purpose: Understand that project management is continuous throughout the lifecycle. Process groups are not sequential but overlap. Monitoring & Controlling runs concurrently with Executing to enable real-time adjustments. Critical for understanding project manager responsibilities at each phase.

diagrams.title: Stakeholder Power/Interest Grid
diagrams.description: Matrix mapping stakeholders based on their power (ability to influence project) and interest (level of concern about outcomes). Four quadrants: Manage Closely (high power, high interest), Keep Satisfied (high power, low interest), Keep Informed (low power, high interest), Monitor (low power, low interest).
elements: Grid axes: Power (horizontal, low to high), Interest (vertical, low to high)
Four quadrants with stakeholder examples
Project Manager position in center
Arrows showing potential stakeholder movement
Color coding: Red (Manage Closely), Yellow (Keep Satisfied), Green (Keep Informed), Blue (Monitor)
diagrams.purpose: Prioritize stakeholder engagement and communication. Different quadrants require different management approaches. Critical for managing client expectations, securing executive support, and ensuring user buy-in. Prevents communication failures that lead to project failure.

diagrams.title: Software Project Success vs Failure Factors
diagrams.description: Bar chart showing CHAOS Report statistics: 29% successful projects, 42% failed projects, 29% challenged projects. Also shows top failure factors with their impact percentages: User input (12.5%), Expectations (9%), Executive support (8%), Requirements changes (8%), Poor PM (7%), etc.
elements: Pie chart: Success (29%), Challenged (29%), Failed (42%)
Bar chart of failure factors with percentages
Callout showing that most failures relate to management, not technology
Trend arrow showing improvement with better PM practices
diagrams.purpose: Understand empirical reality of software project outcomes. Reinforce that management issues—not technical issues—are primary failure causes. Justifies investment in professional project management. Provides concrete evidence for stakeholder discussions about PM importance.
